findings.title: Stale runCheck() can set networkMismatch AFTER disconnect resets it
findings.description: When the wallet disconnects, two effects keyed on [connected] fire in the same React render cycle. The walletDisconnect effect (line 292) calls setNetworkMismatch(null) synchronously. The network-check effect (line 305) launched runCheck() on the previous connect; runCheck() is still awaiting wallet.getNetworkId(). When getNetworkId() eventually resolves, runCheck() calls setNetworkMismatch(result.match ? null : result) on the now-disconnected session. React accepts the setState because the component is still mounted. If the wallet was on the wrong network, networkMismatch is now non-null even though no wallet is connected. On the NEXT connect, the auto-auth effect fires with networkMismatch already set to the stale mismatch, which causes auto-auth to skip. The network-check effect will eventually overwrite it, but there is a window during the new connect cycle where auto-auth is incorrectly blocked by the previous session's stale data.
findings.evidence: walletDisconnect effect (line 292) and network-check effect (line 329) both have [connected] as the sole dependency that triggers on disconnect. runCheck() is void-launched (line 329) with no cancellation token or mounted-check. After disconnect, the async getNetworkId() resolution writes back to shared state with no guard on connected.
findings.fix: Capture a cancel flag in the network-check effect's closure and check it before calling setNetworkMismatch: let cancelled = false; ... if (!cancelled) setNetworkMismatch(...); return () => { cancelled = true; }. Alternatively, add a !connected guard inside runCheck() after awaiting getNetworkId().

findings.title: Auto-auth fires before network check completes on first connect
findings.description: On wallet connect, React runs all effects keyed on [connected] in registration order in the same synchronous pass: validateJWT, walletDisconnect, network-check, then auto-auth. The network-check effect starts runCheck() as a void async call (line 329) and returns immediately. The auto-auth effect then runs synchronously with networkMismatch still null (its initial value). The guard at line 362 is (networkMismatch && !networkMismatch.match) — null is falsy, so it does not block. Auto-auth proceeds to call authenticate(). authenticate() has a belt-and-braces re-check that calls checkWalletNetwork again (line 400), so signData() is never actually called on a mismatched wallet. The bug is therefore mitigated but the guard in auto-auth is semantically misleading: null means both 'no mismatch confirmed' AND 'check not yet run', and the auto-auth guard does not distinguish them.
findings.evidence: React useEffect hooks run in registration order in the same commit. Network-check effect registered at line 305, auto-auth at line 335. runCheck() is async; auto-auth runs before it resolves. networkMismatch initial state is null (line 170); auto-auth guard treats null as 'no mismatch'.
findings.fix: Consider a third state value (e.g., undefined = 'check pending', null = 'match confirmed', WalletNetworkResult = 'mismatch') so auto-auth can explicitly skip when the check is still in flight. With that model the auto-auth guard becomes: if (networkMismatch === undefined || (networkMismatch && !networkMismatch.match)) return. The belt-and-braces check in authenticate() already handles the narrow window safely, so this is a robustness improvement rather than a critical fix.

findings.title: authenticate() early-return on mismatch does not clear popupBlocked
findings.description: When authenticate() detects a network mismatch via its belt-and-braces check (lines 401-404), it calls setNetworkMismatch(networkResult) and setAuthError(null) and returns. It does NOT call setPopupBlocked(false). If a user previously triggered a popup-blocked state (popupBlocked === true), then switched their wallet to the wrong network and clicked Retry, authenticate() would return early due to mismatch while leaving popupBlocked=true. The auto-auth effect would then see popupBlocked=true and skip (line 358), so subsequent reconnect flow would require a manual user action even after the network mismatch is resolved, because popupBlocked is not cleared until disconnect. In practice, popupBlocked and a mismatched network co-occurring requires an unusual sequence, but the state machine is inconsistent.
findings.evidence: Early-return path at line 403: only setNetworkMismatch and setAuthError are called. setPopupBlocked(false) appears only at line 414 inside the normal authentication flow, not in the mismatch early-return path.
findings.fix: Add setPopupBlocked(false) to the mismatch early-return block at line 403, alongside the existing setAuthError(null).

residual_risks.description: The timeout leaves a live 45-second setTimeout after signData() succeeds. The rejected promise is already settled so the reject call is a no-op, and there is no console pollution or state mutation. The timer is a minor GC-delay (one timer object per sign operation, freed after 45s). This is acceptable behavior and not a bug.

If signData() resolves after the 45-second timeout wins the race, the Mesh SDK promise resolves with no listener. React state is not mutated because the catch block has already run and isAuthenticating has been set to false. Safe.

networkMismatch === null is used for both 'check passed' and 'getNetworkId() threw'. The plan explicitly accepts this design. The auto-auth effect's null-pass-through is consistent with this decision, and authenticate()'s belt-and-braces check catches real mismatches that slip through on error.

testing_gaps.description: No test covers the disconnect-while-check-in-flight scenario (COR-001). A test would connect a wallet, start the async network check, immediately disconnect, then assert that networkMismatch is null after disconnect and remains null through the subsequent reconnect. Without this, the stale-write window will not be caught by regression.

No test covers the ordering gap between network-check and auto-auth effects (COR-002). A test would mock getNetworkId() to return after a deliberate delay and assert that authenticate() is called at most once, and only after the network check resolves with a match.
